data.title: Treasury Wine Suspends Dividend After Large Half-Year Loss
data.description:
(21:20 GMT) *Treasury Wine 1H Statutory Net Loss A$649.4 Mln
(21:21 GMT) *Treasury Wine 1H Profit Pre-Material Items, SGARA A$128.5 Mln, Down 46%
(21:22 GMT) *Treasury Wine 1H Earnings Before Interest, Tax and Other Items A$236.4 Mln, Down 40%
(21:22 GMT) *Treasury Wine 1H Revenue A$1.3 Bln, Down 17%
(21:23 GMT) *Treasury Wine Suspends Interim Dividend
(21:24 GMT) *Treasury Wine: Suspension of Dividend is Temporary Measure to Prioritize Capital Preservation, Reduce Leverage
(21:25 GMT) *Treasury Wine: Resumption of Dividends Will be Subject to Financial Performance
(21:25 GMT) *Treasury Wine: Underlying Performance of Key Brands Remains Positive
(21:26 GMT) *Treasury Wine: Expects 2H Ebits to be Higher Than 1H
(21:27 GMT) *Treasury Wine: 2H Ebits To be Driven by Improved Momentum in California Following Completion of Distributor Transition
(21:47 GMT) Treasury Wine Suspends Dividend After Large Half-Year Loss

By Mike Cherney

SYDNEY--Australia-based Treasury Wine Estates suspended its dividend after posting a large half-year loss, reflecting the vintner's struggles as it battled tough market conditions in the U.S. and China.

Treasury, which owns the iconic Australian brand Penfolds and DAOU and Frank Family Vineyards in the U.S., said its net loss for the half year ended in December was 649.4 million Australian dollars (US$458.3 million), reflecting a previously announced write-down on its U.S. assets. That compares to a A$220.9 million profit in the same period the prior year.

Treasury said its key earnings metric, known as Ebits, was A$236.4 million, down 40%.

The company said it wouldn't pay an interim dividend. It characterized the suspension of dividends as a temporary measure aimed at preserving capital and reducing leverage, and said the resumption of dividends would be dependent on financial performance.

Treasury posted earnings declines in all its key divisions, with Penfolds down 20%, its Americas unit down 64% and its Treasury Collective unit down 51%.

Looking ahead, Treasury said it expected its fiscal second half Ebits to be higher than the first half, driven by improved momentum in California following completion of a distributor transition.

The Australia-based vintner has had a rough few months, as it battled uncertain demand in China, a flagging wine market in the U.S. and a change to its California distribution. It scrapped its initial annual guidance and said it would write down the value of its U.S. assets. It also paused and later canceled a share buyback, said it would reduce customer inventory holdings in the U.S. and China, and launched a cost-cutting effort.

One big headwind for the Treasury has been China's attempt to rein in excess among its civil servants, which has hit demand for wine. In the U.S., luxury wine market trends have continued to moderate and the premium wine segment remains in decline, Treasury has said.

Treasury had shifted toward higher-margin luxury products amid waning global appetite for budget brands, but some analysts have expressed concerns that the write-down on its U.S. assets indicated that it overpaid for acquisitions.

There has been some momentum in recent days, however. Treasury said last week that it would slightly beat its prior guidance for the fiscal first half, and that it had settled a dispute with one of its U.S. distributors--though that involved Treasury repurchasing inventory held by the distributor.

Despite a bump last week, the vintner's shares have been on a downward slide. Shares closed Friday at A$5.24, down 33% since August and 53% below where they were at the end of 2024.

Write to Mike Cherney at mike.cherney@wsj.com

data.title: Treasury Wine's first-half profit halves, interim dividend suspended
data.description:
Australia's Treasury Wine Estates' first-half underlying profit nearly halved on Monday, and it temporarily suspended the payment of its fiscal 2026 interim dividend as a measure to prioritise preservation of capital.

The result underscores a reset in its two largest markets after 2025 shipments ran ahead of underlying demand, leaving elevated distributor inventories in China and the U.S. that management has moved to unwind.

The firm reported a 46.3% slump in net profit after tax before material items and SGARA of A$128.5 million ($90.85 million) for the six months ended December 31, down from A$239.6 million a year ago.

That slightly beat the Visible Alpha consensus estimate of A$127.8 million.

Adding to concerns, one of Treasury Wine's U.S. distributors shut down in California, forcing a scramble for new partners and leading to a 28.4% drop in first-half net sales revenue at Treasury Americas to A$283 million.

($1 = 1.4144 Australian dollars)

data.title: OML: Revenue up 9%, NPAT down 54% on NZ impairment, dividend up 19%, strong cost control
data.description:
Revenue grew 9% to $691.4 million with strong first-half performance, but statutory NPAT fell 54% due to a $30 million New Zealand impairment. Dividend increased 19% to 6.25c per share, and the company maintained a robust balance sheet and delivered $15 million in cost savings.

Original document: oOh media Ltd [OML] Annual Report — Feb. 15 2026

data.title: Australian Stocks on Course for Opening Rise — Market Talk
data.description: Australian equities look set to resume their recent rise at the start of the trading week. Local stock futures are up by 0.6% ahead of Monday's session, suggesting the S&P/ASX 200 is on course for an opening bounce. The benchmark index fell 1.4% Friday but still completed its best week since May 2025, rising 2.4% across five sessions. Ahead of the open, real-estate group Stockland reported a 19% rise in first-half profit. Bendigo & Adelaide Bank posted a 6.4% rise in first-half profit, while PPE manufacturer Ansell raised its dividend. U.S. equities provided a mixed lead at the end of last week. The DJIA added 0.1%, the S&P 500 finished less than 0.1%, and the Nasdaq Composite slipped 0.2%. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

data.title: Australia's BlueScope posts two-fold jump in first-half earnings
data.description:
Australia's BlueScope on Monday reported a more than two-fold jump in its first-half earnings due to stronger U.S. spreads, higher volumes across key markets, and solid cost performance.

The steelmaker posted underlying net profit after tax of A$382 million ($270.07 million), compared to A$176.4 million last year.

($1 = 1.4144 Australian dollars)

data.description:

(21:23 GMT) *JB Hi-Fi 1H Revenue A$6.09 Billion, Up 7.3% on Year
(21:24 GMT) *JB Hi-Fi Interim Dividend A$2.10/Share
(21:27 GMT) *JB Hi-Fi 1H Ebit A$454.0 Million, Up 8.1% on Year
(21:28 GMT) *JB Hi-Fi Says Total Sales for JB Hi-Fi Australia Up 4.0% in January
(21:28 GMT) *JB Hi-Fi Says Like-for-Like Sales for JB Hi-Fi Australia Up 2.4% in January
(21:28 GMT) *JB Hi-Fi Says Like-for-Like Sales for JB Hi-Fi New Zealand Up 17% in January
(21:29 GMT) *JB Hi-Fi Says Total Sales for JB Hi-Fi New Zealand Up 26% in January
(21:29 GMT) *JB Hi-Fi Says Total Sales for The Good Guys Up 2.7% in January
(21:29 GMT) *JB Hi-Fi Says Like-for-Like Sales for The Good Guys Up 2.7% in January
(21:29 GMT) *JB Hi-Fi Says Like-for-Like Sales for e&s Down 7.9% in January
(21:29 GMT) *JB Hi-Fi Says Total Sales Sales for e&s Down 4.6% in January
(21:34 GMT) JB Hi-Fi 1H Net Profit Rises 7.1%, Sales Growth Continues in January

By David Winning

SYDNEY--Electronics retailer JB Hi-Fi said its half-year net profit rose by 7.1% after it achieved record sales, and the performance of its major brands has continued to improve in January.

JB Hi-Fi reported a net profit of 305.8 million Australian dollars (US$216.3 million) for the six months through December, up from A$285.4 million a year earlier. Revenue rose by 7.3% to A$6.09 billion in the period.

Directors of the company declared an interim dividend of A$2.10/share, representing a payout ratio of 75% of net profit. JB Hi-Fi has increased the dividend payout ratio to a range of 70-80% of net profit, from 65%, starting from fiscal 2026.

JB Hi-Fi said same-store sales of its own-name stores in Australia rose by 2.4% in January, and were up 16.7% in New Zealand. Comparable sales at The Good Guys lifted by 2.7% last month.

"Whilst we are pleased to see sales growth continue in January in JB Hi-Fi and The Good Guys, cycling strong sales in the prior year, we remain cautious given the uncertainty in the retail market and the continued competitive activity," Chief Executive Nick Wells said.

Write to David Winning at david.winning@wsj.com

data.title: Warner Bros. Lands Ninth Straight Box Office Winner With 'Wuthering Heights' — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Janet H. Cho

Warner Bros. Discovery's Wuthering Heights notched the studio's ninth-straight number one box office debut over the long holiday weekend, with its domestic tally expected to reach $40 million through Monday.

Wuthering Heights is the biggest opening of the year so far, raking in an estimated $34.8 million in domestic box office sales over the three-day weekend, according to Comscore. The projected total sales through Monday, also from Comscore, includes $4.8 million globally on IMAX screens.

For Hollywood overall, estimated weekend box office sales of $119.2 million through Sunday are down 23% from last year, when Walt Disney's Captain America: Brave New World opened to $88.8 million over the three-day weekend and more than $100 million over the four-day holiday weekend, according to Comscore. But this year's estimated year-to-date domestic box office of $899.8 million is up 8.1% compared with the same point in 2025, Dergarabedian said.

Timing was everything for Wuthering Heights. With Valentine's Day on Saturday, the biggest movie-going day of the week, "there could not be a better date night option with both Margot Robbie and Jacob Elordi promising to bring this modern adaptation of Emily Brontë's 1847 novel classic tale to a swooning audience," Comscore's head of marketplace trends Paul Dergarabedian said.

The film was written and directed by BAFTA-winning filmmaker Emerald Fennell, and drew a largely female audience.

The film garnered significant international interest, earning $42 million internationally for an impressive global debut total of $82 million, Dergarabedian said.

The weekend's No. 2 film was a Sony Pictures' GOAT, a PG-rated animated adventure about a small goat that dreams of playing a full-contact sport called roarball, dominated by the fiercest animals in the world. It stars Caleb McLaughlin, Gabrielle Union, Stephen Curry, Aaron Pierre.

GOAT, for Greatest of All Time, sold an estimated $26 million over the three-day weekend, and is expected to sell $32 million through Monday. It also sold $15.6 million internationally, for a $47.6 million debut weekend.

Opening at No. 3 was Amazon MGM Studios' Crime 101, a thriller starring Chris Hemsworth, Halle Berry, and Mark Ruffalo that sold $15.2 million through Sunday and is projected to sell $17.8 million through Monday.

Eric Handler, senior research analyst at Roth, wrote last week that while Hollywood got off to a good start, with $620 million in revenue, up 14% from the previous January and the best start to the year since 2019, "February is proving more challenging."

Although Hollywood studios are releasing 13 films this month, up from seven titles last year, he does not expect any of them to match last year's $89 million opening of Captain America: Brave New World.

"March should prove strong with two standout hits" in Disney Pixar's animated Hoppers, opening March 6, and Amazon MGM's sci-fi thriller Project Hail Mary, opening March 20, Handler wrote.

Write to Janet H. Cho at janet.cho@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.
